card.intuition: A stateless way to verify a user's identity using a digitally signed JSON object (Token) that the client sends with every request.
card.analogy: A personalized theme park wristband: you show your ID once to get the band, and then you just show the band at every ride. The rides don't need to call the office to check if you're allowed; they just look at the band's signature.
when_to_use: Stateless microservices where you don't want every service querying a session database
Mobile apps where keeping a persistent cookie is difficult
gotchas: JWTs are NOT encrypted by default — anyone can read the data inside; they are only signed to prevent tampering
Revoking a JWT before it expires is difficult because the server doesn't keep track of them (the token is the proof)

steps.label: Login
steps.annotation: Client sends credentials to auth endpoint.

steps.label: Validate
steps.annotation: Server verifies password hash against stored bcrypt hash.

steps.label: Sign JWT
steps.annotation: Server signs JWT with secret (HS256) or private key (RS256). Payload: userId, exp.

steps.label: Return
steps.annotation: Token returned — stored in httpOnly cookie or memory (not localStorage).

steps.label: Request
steps.annotation: Client sends JWT in Authorization header on protected routes.

steps.label: Verify
steps.annotation: Server verifies signature and expiry — stateless, no DB lookup.

exercise.prompt: Identify the three parts of a JWT and the character that separates them.
hints: The first part is the Header
The last part is the Signature
The parts are separated by a dot (.)
exercise.test_code:
assert jwt_structure["part1"].lower() == "header"
assert jwt_structure["part3"].lower() == "signature"
assert jwt_structure["separator"] == "."
print("PASS")
